title: Build Your Safety Net
reasoning: An emergency fund is a prerequisite for all other financial moves. Without 3 months of expenses in cash, any unexpected event forces liquidation of investments — potentially at a loss and with tax consequences. The opportunity cost of keeping cash is real but small; the risk of investing without a buffer is asymmetric and large.
common_mistake: Players choose B because investing feels like the 'smart money move' and emergency funds feel passive. This is present bias applied to risk — the future emergency is discounted because it isn't happening now.
teaching_moment: The emergency fund isn't an investment — it's insurance against being forced to make terrible financial decisions under duress. Its return is measured in bad decisions avoided, not yield.

title: Your First Credit Card
optimal_label: Accept card, pay balance in full monthly
suboptimal_label: Decline the card to avoid debt risk
reasoning: Declining a credit card at this stage leaves a thin file for years. The optimal move is to accept, use for one recurring purchase, and autopay in full — building payment history (35% of FICO) at zero cost. The fear of debt is rational in the abstract but counterproductive when the card is used as a tool, not a loan.
common_mistake: Players decline the card because they associate credit cards with debt. This conflates the instrument with the misuse of the instrument.
teaching_moment: A credit card paid in full every month costs nothing and builds the single most valuable financial asset a young adult can have: a credit history. The card is a tool. The debt comes from the behavior, not the card.

title: Car Decision
optimal_label: Buy reliable used car with cash
reasoning: A new car depreciates 20% in year one. Financing amplifies this: you pay interest on an asset that is actively losing value. A reliable used car (3–5 years old) has absorbed the steepest depreciation curve and can be purchased outright or with minimal financing. The true cost difference over 5 years between a financed new car and a used cash purchase is typically $15,000 to $25,000.
common_mistake: Players choose A because a new car feels like a reward for early financial progress. This is lifestyle inflation — upgrading consumption as income rises without corresponding wealth growth.
teaching_moment: Cars are depreciating liabilities. Every dollar of car payment is a dollar not compounding in investments. The financially optimal vehicle is the one that gets you to work reliably at the lowest total cost of ownership.

title: Medical Bill
suboptimal_label: Pay in full immediately from savings
reasoning: A 0% payment plan on medical debt is mathematically superior to depleting savings. The opportunity cost of liquidating savings (lost HYSA interest, lost investment time) exceeds zero. The 0% rate fundamentally changes the calculus — this is not the same as carrying high-interest credit card debt. The only scenario where paying in full wins: if you are certain you will miss payments and trigger penalty APR.
common_mistake: Players choose A because paying in full feels psychologically clean. The discomfort of an outstanding obligation overrides the financial logic — this is loss aversion operating on the debt side.
teaching_moment: 0% debt is free money. The bank or hospital is lending you their money at no cost. Refusing it by paying immediately is paying a premium for the psychological comfort of zero balance.

title: First Investing Decision
optimal_label: Open Roth IRA, invest in index fund
suboptimal_label: Keep saving in HYSA for now
reasoning: At this career stage, the player has an established emergency fund and manageable debt. The Roth IRA window — while in a lower tax bracket — is the highest-value move available. Index fund returns historically outpace HYSA rates by 4–5% annually. The tax-free compounding on Roth contributions made in your 20s is irreplaceable.
common_mistake: Players choose B because the HYSA feels safe and the stock market feels risky. This is availability heuristic applied to market risk — recent volatility is more salient than 30 years of index fund data.
teaching_moment: Risk is not just the chance of loss. Keeping money in a savings account earning 4.5% while inflation runs at 3% is a guaranteed 1.5% real return — while a diversified index fund has returned 7% real over every 20-year period in history.

title: 401k Match
optimal_label: Contribute enough to capture full employer match
suboptimal_label: Skip 401k, pay down student loans faster
reasoning: Employer match is a guaranteed 50–100% instant return on contributions — no investment product competes with this. Skipping the match to pay off low-interest student loans (under 7%) is mathematically inferior in all but extreme debt situations. The match plus compounding over 30 years dwarfs the interest saved on student loans.
common_mistake: Players choose B because student loan debt feels urgent and the 401k match feels abstract and distant. This is present bias — the future benefit of the match is discounted relative to the immediate satisfaction of reducing debt.
teaching_moment: The 401k match has a cutoff date — the payroll year. Unclaimed match is permanently gone. Student loan interest is a fixed cost that will be there next month. Always capture the match first.

title: Market Crash Response
suboptimal_label: Move investments to cash until market recovers
reasoning: Selling during a market downturn locks in losses and requires correctly timing both the exit AND the re-entry. Studies show investors who moved to cash during the 2008 and 2020 crashes and waited for 'recovery signals' missed 50–80% of the rebound, which happened in the first few weeks. Time in market beats timing the market in 90%+ of historical cases.
common_mistake: Players choose A because the loss feels unbearable and action feels better than inaction. This is loss aversion and availability heuristic combined — the crash is vivid and current, recovery is abstract.
teaching_moment: A market drop only becomes a loss when you sell. Paper losses are temporary for diversified long-term investors. The investor who holds through a 40% crash and recovers ends up wealthier than the investor who sold at the bottom and bought back after the recovery.

title: Side Income Tax Planning
optimal_label: Set aside 30% quarterly, pay estimated taxes
suboptimal_label: Pay it all at filing in April
reasoning: 1099 income requires quarterly estimated payments. Paying all at April filing triggers underpayment penalties that compound per quarter, plus a large cash crunch. Systematic quarterly payments smooth cash flow and eliminate penalties. The 30% set-aside creates a tax savings habit that also disciplines spending.
common_mistake: Players choose B because the tax bill isn't due yet and the quarterly process feels complex. This is present bias — the future penalty is discounted, and the complexity of quarterly filing triggers avoidance.
teaching_moment: Self-employed income has no withholding. Every dollar earned is gross. The IRS considers quarterly payments a legal obligation, not a courtesy — missing them incurs penalties even when you pay in full in April.

title: Windfall Decision
optimal_label: Max IRA and invest remainder in index fund
reasoning: Mortgage rates in a typical environment (4–7%) are below expected market returns (7% real). Paying down a 5% mortgage early is a guaranteed 5% return. Investing in a diversified index fund historically returns 7%+ real. The math favors investing, especially when the mortgage also provides a tax deduction and the investments compound tax-advantaged in a Roth IRA.
common_mistake: Players choose B because debt-free feels better than invested. Mortgage payoff is emotionally salient and concrete; investment returns are probabilistic and abstract. This is present bias plus loss aversion.
teaching_moment: When your borrowing rate is lower than your expected investment return, paying down debt early is giving up expected wealth to buy psychological comfort. That comfort has a calculable price — and it's usually in the tens of thousands of dollars.

title: Retirement Account Strategy
optimal_label: Maximize tax-advantaged accounts before taxable investing
suboptimal_label: Invest in taxable brokerage for flexibility
reasoning: Tax-advantaged space (401k, IRA, HSA) is limited and non-recoverable year-to-year. A taxable brokerage provides flexibility but at the cost of annual taxes on dividends and capital gains, and no deduction on contributions. For long-term wealth building, exhausting tax-advantaged space before taxable investing is the dominant strategy in all but a few edge cases (very high income, early retirement before 59.5).
common_mistake: Players choose B because taxable accounts feel more flexible and accessible. Roth IRAs actually allow contribution withdrawal at any time without penalty — making the flexibility argument mostly moot.
teaching_moment: Tax drag on a taxable brokerage is invisible but enormous. A 7% return in a taxable account becomes approximately 5.5–6% after taxes on dividends and gains. Over 30 years on $10,000, that gap is over $50,000.
